g.data-narration: Welcome to Leftist Trees - a powerful data structure that makes merging priority queues efficient.
g.text: Efficient Priority Queue Operations with O(log n) Merge

g.data-narration: Binary heaps can't merge efficiently - they take O(n) time. Leftist trees solve this with a clever structure.
The key insight: leftist trees are biased to the left, making the right spine shortest path always O(log n).
g.text: For every node: s(left) ≥ s(right) — the right spine is always the shortest path!

g.data-narration: The s-value represents the shortest path from a node to an external null node. Let me show you how to compute it.
g.text: Shortest path from node x to an external node (NULL)

g.data-narration: Let's compute s-values for this tree. Notice how we work from bottom to top - leaves first, then parents.
The leftist property ensures s(left) is always greater than or equal to s(right). This makes right spine short!
A min-leftist tree combines the leftist property with min-heap: root is always the minimum element.
Here's how we represent a leftist node in C - with key, s-value, and left/right pointers.
The merge operation is the heart of leftist trees. Let's understand its steps before diving into the code.
Here's the complete merge function in C. Notice how it recursively merges along the right spine only.
Insert is simple - just create a single node and merge it with the existing tree!
g.text: Create single-node tree → merge with root
Merge left and right subtrees of root

g.data-narration: Let's trace through merging two trees. H1 has root 3, H2 has root 6. Ready to merge step by step!
Step 1: Compare roots 3 and 6 → 3 is smaller, becomes root. Step 2: Merge H1's right (8) with H2 (6).
g.text: 3 < 6 → 3 is new root. Recursively merge right subtree (8) with H2
6 < 8 → 6 is root. Recursively merge right subtree (7) with subtree(8)

g.data-narration: Step 3: Compare 7 vs 8 → 7 is smaller. Step 4: Merge 7's right (NULL) with 8, then check s-values and swap!
g.text: 7 < 8 → 7 is root. Merge right(NULL) with 8 → returns 8

g.data-narration: Step 5: At node 6, no swap needed. Step 6: At node 3, s(left)=1 < s(right)=2 → SWAP!
Here's the final merged tree! Notice it's a valid min-leftist tree with all s-values correct.
All operations are O(log n) because the right spine is always at most log₂(n+1) long.
When should you use leftist trees? When you need to merge priority queues frequently!
To summarize: leftist trees excel at merging priority queues with O(log n) time. Remember the s-value rules and merge algorithm!
g.text: • Merge is the fundamental operation - all others use it
• Leftist property: s(left) ≥ s(right) → right spine ≤ log₂(n+1)
EXAM TIP: Practice tracing merge step-by-step!

g.data-narration: Leftist trees are used in external sorting, operating system timers, and graph algorithms that need mergeable priority queues.
